exercises.title: Three attribution dimensions on day one
exercises.prompt: Traditional FinOps (tags + tiers) breaks on LLM spend: costs are token-transactions, not resource-uptime. The 2026 playbook instruments three dimensions on day one: per-user (user_id, for seat pricing), per-task (task_id+route, for product-surface cost), per-tenant (tenant_id, for unit economics). Build a cost attributor that tags every call with all three and rolls up spend by each dimension. Show the same spend attributed three ways.
exercises.hint: Each call: {user_id, task_id, route, tenant_id, cost}. Roll up: sum by user_id, by task_id, by tenant_id. Show one spend stream attributed three ways -> each answers a different business question (seats, product surfaces, unit economics).
exercises.solution:
Traditional FinOps tags resources (a server, a disk) — but an LLM call is a *transaction*, not an asset, so tags do not map. The fix is to attribute every call across three business dimensions from day one.

```python
from collections import defaultdict

def attribute(calls):
    by_user, by_task, by_tenant = defaultdict(float), defaultdict(float), defaultdict(float)
    for c in calls:
        by_user[c['user_id']] += c['cost']
        by_task[c['route']] += c['cost']
        by_tenant[c['tenant_id']] += c['cost']
    return by_user, by_task, by_tenant

calls = [
    {'user_id': 'u1', 'route': 'summarize', 'tenant_id': 'acme', 'cost': 0.5},
    {'user_id': 'u2', 'route': 'chat', 'tenant_id': 'acme', 'cost': 0.3},
    {'user_id': 'u1', 'route': 'summarize', 'tenant_id': 'beta', 'cost': 0.7},
]
bu, bt, bten = attribute(calls)
print('by user (seat pricing)  :', dict(bu))
print('by route (product cost) :', dict(bt))
print('by tenant (unit econ)   :', dict(bten))
```

Output:

```
by user (seat pricing)  : {'u1': 1.2, 'u2': 0.3}
by route (product cost) : {'summarize': 1.2, 'chat': 0.3}
by tenant (unit econ)   : {'acme': 0.8, 'beta': 0.7}
```

**Why three dimensions, why day one.** The *same* spend answers three different business questions depending on how you slice it: **per-user** (u1 costs $1.20) drives seat pricing and expansion decisions; **per-task/route** (summarize costs $1.20) tells you which product surfaces are expensive and worth prioritizing or optimizing; **per-tenant** (acme $0.80, beta $0.70) is your unit economics for renewal and margin. One bucket — "total LLM spend" — hides all of this. And the lesson is emphatic: instrument at *request creation*, day one, because **retroactive tagging always misses** — you cannot recover the user/task/tenant of a call you already made without the tags. The four token layers (prompt, tool, memory, response) must each be attributed too, or one layer hides spend. The next exercise builds the unit metric on top of this.

from collections import defaultdict

def attribute(calls):
    # Sum cost three ways: by user_id, by route, by tenant_id. Return the three
    # dicts (by_user, by_route, by_tenant).
    pass

exercises.title: Cost per resolved query, not dollars per million tokens
exercises.prompt: The right unit metric is cost-per-outcome (cost per resolved query, cost per generated artifact), NOT $/M tokens. Build a calculator that, given a workflow's token spend across its layers (prompt, tool, memory, response) and the number of successful outcomes, computes cost-per-resolved-query — and show why $/M tokens misleads: a cheaper-per-token model that needs more retries can have a HIGHER cost per resolved query.
exercises.hint: cost_per_query = total_token_cost / successful_outcomes. Compare model A (cheap/token but low success -> more retries) vs model B (pricier/token but high success). Show A has lower $/M tokens but HIGHER cost per resolved query because of retries/failures.
exercises.solution:
The standard LLM cost metric — dollars per million tokens — measures the *wrong thing*. What matters to the business is cost per *outcome*, and a cheaper-per-token model can cost more per resolved query once you count retries and failures.

```python
def cost_per_resolved(token_cost_per_m, tokens_per_attempt, success_rate, queries):
    # failed attempts retry until success -> attempts = queries / success_rate
    attempts = queries / success_rate
    total_tokens = attempts * tokens_per_attempt
    total_cost = total_tokens / 1e6 * token_cost_per_m
    return total_cost / queries

queries = 1000
# Model A: cheap per token, but lower success -> more retries
a = cost_per_resolved(0.40, 2000, success_rate=0.6, queries=queries)
# Model B: pricier per token, but high success -> fewer retries
b = cost_per_resolved(2.00, 2000, success_rate=0.95, queries=queries)
print(f"Model A: $0.40/M tokens, 60% success -> ${a:.4f} per resolved query")
print(f"Model B: $2.00/M tokens, 95% success -> ${b:.4f} per resolved query")
print(f"cheaper per TOKEN: A. cheaper per OUTCOME: {'A' if a < b else 'B'}")
```

Output:

```
Model A: $0.40/M tokens, 60% success -> $0.0013 per resolved query
Model B: $2.00/M tokens, 95% success -> $0.0042 per resolved query
```

With these numbers A still wins per outcome — but tighten the success gap and the ranking flips:

```python
a2 = cost_per_resolved(0.40, 2000, 0.30, queries)   # very low success
b2 = cost_per_resolved(0.55, 2000, 0.95, queries)   # slightly pricier, reliable
print(f"A2: $0.40/M, 30% success -> ${a2:.4f}/query")
print(f"B2: $0.55/M, 95% success -> ${b2:.4f}/query  ({'B2 cheaper per outcome' if b2<a2 else 'A2'})")
```

Output:

```
A2: $0.40/M, 30% success -> $0.0027/query
B2: $0.55/M, 95% success -> $0.0012/query  (B2 cheaper per outcome)
```

**Why $/M tokens misleads.** A model's per-token price ignores how many tokens you *actually spend* to get a result. A cheap-per-token model with low success retries repeatedly — each retry burns the full token cost again — so its cost *per resolved query* can exceed a pricier-but-reliable model. The second comparison makes it concrete: model A2 is cheapest per token ($0.40/M) but its 30% success rate means ~3.3 attempts per resolution, while B2 ($0.55/M, 95% success) resolves in ~1.05 attempts and wins on cost-per-outcome. Choosing the model with the lowest $/M would pick the *more expensive* option per actual result.

**The unit metric that matters.** Cost per resolved query (or per generated artifact) is the business-relevant number because it ties spend to *value delivered*, not tokens consumed. It naturally accounts for retries, failures, and the multi-layer token spend (prompt + tool + memory + response). This is why model routing (Lesson 16) gates on *quality*, not just per-token price — a cheap model that fails is not cheap. The final exercise covers enforcement when spend goes wrong.

    # Failed attempts retry: attempts = queries/success_rate. Return total token
    # cost divided by queries (cost per RESOLVED query).
    pass

tests.desc: the formula resolves to the per-query token cost
lower success raises cost per resolved query

exercises.title: The multi-tenant enforcement ladder with kill switches
exercises.prompt: Multi-tenant FinOps needs an enforcement ladder: rate limits per tenant (2-3x expected peak), daily spend caps (1.5-3x contracted ceiling, tighten + alert), kill switches on spend anomaly (z-score > 4 -> auto-pause + page). Build this three-rung ladder and show a tenant escalating through it: normal traffic passes, sustained overage tightens limits, and a sudden spend spike (z-score>4) triggers the kill switch. Show each rung activating.
exercises.hint: Track tenant spend. Rung 1: rate limit at 2-3x expected peak (429 over that). Rung 2: daily cap at 1.5-3x contracted -> tighten rate + alert. Rung 3: compute spend z-score vs baseline; if >4, kill switch (auto-pause + page). Show a tenant going normal -> overage (tighten) -> spike (kill).
exercises.solution:
Multi-tenant cost control is a graduated *ladder*, not a single limit: each rung responds to a worse situation, ending in a kill switch for true anomalies. This bounds spend at multiple time scales without over-reacting to normal variation.

```python
import statistics

class EnforcementLadder:
    def __init__(self, expected_peak, contracted_daily, baseline_spends):
        self.rate_limit = expected_peak * 2.5        # rung 1
        self.daily_cap = contracted_daily * 2.0      # rung 2
        self.mean = statistics.mean(baseline_spends)  # rung 3 baseline
        self.std = statistics.pstdev(baseline_spends)

    def check(self, current_rps, daily_spend, latest_spend):
        # rung 3 (most severe): spend anomaly kill switch
        z = (latest_spend - self.mean) / self.std if self.std else 0
        if z > 4:
            return f"RUNG 3 KILL SWITCH: spend z-score {z:.1f} > 4 -> auto-pause + page on-call"
        # rung 2: daily cap
        if daily_spend > self.daily_cap:
            return f"RUNG 2: daily cap ${self.daily_cap:.0f} exceeded -> tighten rate + alert"
        # rung 1: rate limit
        if current_rps > self.rate_limit:
            return f"RUNG 1: rate limit {self.rate_limit:.0f} RPS exceeded -> 429"
        return 'normal: passes all rungs'

ladder = EnforcementLadder(expected_peak=100, contracted_daily=500,
                           baseline_spends=[480, 510, 495, 505, 490])
print(ladder.check(current_rps=80, daily_spend=400, latest_spend=495))   # normal
print(ladder.check(current_rps=300, daily_spend=400, latest_spend=495))  # rung 1
print(ladder.check(current_rps=80, daily_spend=1100, latest_spend=495))  # rung 2
print(ladder.check(current_rps=80, daily_spend=400, latest_spend=5000))  # rung 3
```

Output:

```
normal: passes all rungs
RUNG 1: rate limit 250 RPS exceeded -> 429
RUNG 2: daily cap $1000 exceeded -> tighten rate + alert
RUNG 3 KILL SWITCH: spend z-score ... > 4 -> auto-pause + page on-call
```

**Three rungs, three severities.** Each rung responds proportionally: **Rung 1 (rate limit, 2-3x expected peak)** is routine — a tenant briefly exceeding its normal RPS gets clean 429s, no human involved. **Rung 2 (daily cap, 1.5-3x contracted)** is a sustained-overage signal — tighten the rate and *alert* a human, because the tenant is using far more than contracted and someone should look. **Rung 3 (kill switch on spend z-score > 4)** is a true anomaly — a $5000 spend against a ~$495 baseline is 4+ standard deviations out, almost certainly a runaway loop, a bug, or an attack; auto-pause immediately and *page* on-call. The graduated response avoids both extremes: not so trigger-happy that normal bursts page someone (rung 1 handles those silently), not so lax that a runaway burns the budget (rung 3 catches it in seconds).

**Why z-score for the kill switch.** Fixed thresholds are hard to set across heterogeneous tenants (a big tenant's normal spend is a small tenant's anomaly). A z-score against *each tenant's own baseline* adapts automatically — "4 standard deviations above this tenant's normal" is anomalous regardless of the tenant's absolute size. This is the same statistical-anomaly detection as the kill-switch lesson in Phase 15, applied to spend. It catches the "Denial of Wallet" runaway (an agent that found a loop and keeps billing) that fixed daily caps would catch only *after* a full day's budget is gone.

**The FinOps synthesis.** LLM FinOps requires instrumenting three attribution dimensions at request creation (Exercise 1, because retroactive tagging always misses), measuring cost per *outcome* not per token (Exercise 2, because a cheap-per-token model that retries is not cheap), and enforcing a graduated ladder — rate limits, daily caps, anomaly kill switches — to bound multi-tenant spend at every time scale (this exercise). Traditional FinOps breaks because LLM costs are token-transactions tied to engineering decisions (prompt design, context size, output length, retries are *financial* decisions). The fix is treating cost as a first-class, attributed, outcome-denominated, actively-enforced metric — which, combined with caching (Lesson 14), batch (Lesson 15), and routing (Lesson 16), is what makes an LLM product economically sustainable at scale.

        self.mean = statistics.mean(baseline_spends)
        self.std = statistics.pstdev(baseline_spends)
    def check(self, current_rps, daily_spend, latest_spend):
        # Rung 3 (z-score > 4) kill switch; else rung 2 (daily cap); else rung 1
        # (rate limit); else 'normal ...'. Return the matching message.
        pass

tests.desc: a quiet tenant passes all rungs
a blown daily cap trips rung 2
a spend anomaly trips the kill switch
